message.content:
Репозиторий: /Users/madiever/GenTestMVP (монорепо: server/ Node+TS+Express+Mongoose+OpenAI; client/ React+Vite — это админка).
Ревью изменений ветки feat/knowledge-bank поверх базы 6431bf3..HEAD (2 коммита: админ-UI банка + переменный размер теста и качество вопросов).
Сначала прочитай диапазон диффа:  git -C /Users/madiever/GenTestMVP diff 6431bf3 HEAD -- <относящиеся пути>
Затем читай окружающий код, чтобы понять контекст. Ищи РЕАЛЬНЫЕ дефекты корректности и интеграции, а не стиль.
Контекст фич:
 - Бэкенд KC/банк (questionBank.service.ts, ktp.controller.ts, ktp.routes.ts): новый read-эндпоинт listItems вопросов банка (admin); size-параметр assembleNodeTest (разрешено 5/10/15/20), size подмешан в реюз-хэш; generateForCoverage переписан на циклы раундов + разброс difficulty; contentHashOf теперь хеширует текст+варианты+правильный ответ.
 - Test.model.ts: валидатор questions.length сменён с ===10 на диапазон 1..120.
 - test.routes.ts/test.controller.ts: /tests/node-bank принимает size; /generate(+guest) валидируют questionCount.
 - client: ktp.api.ts (+методы KC/банка), ktp.types.ts (+типы), components/ktp/TopicKnowledgeBank.tsx (новый), KtpCatalogPage.tsx (врезка per-topic), гейтинг по role==='admin'.
Возвращай только обоснованные находки с file/location/severity/detail/suggestedFix.

Адверсариально ПРОВЕРЬ находку — попробуй её ОПРОВЕРГНУТЬ, прочитав реальный код. Если это не настоящий дефект (ложноположительное, или уже корректно обрабатывается), верни isReal=false. По умолчанию при сомнении — isReal=false.

Находка:
{
  "file": "server/src/services/questionBank.service.ts",
  "location": "generateForCoverage, цикл раундов (строки 182-255), переменная rejected",
  "severity": "low",
  "title": "rejected суммируется по всем раундам — один и тот же дефицит может многократно отвергаться, цифра завышает реальный брак",
  "detail": "rejected инкрементируется внутри цикла раундов и не сбрасывается между раундами. Если KC хронически недозаполняется (верификатор режет большую часть), один и тот же дефицит генерируется в раундах 1..3, и каждый отказ считается заново — возвращаемое created/rejected перекошено в сторону rejected. Это не вызывает бесконечный цикл (producedThisRound===0 -> break корректно останавливает при нулевом прогрессе, дубликаты 11000 не инкрементят producedThisRound, что верно), и не приводит к двойному учёту created. Дефект чисто отчётный: число rejected в ответе и в логах больше, чем уникальных отвергнутых вопросов, что может ввести в заблуждение админ-UI качества.",
  "suggestedFix": "Либо считать rejected как множество уникальных отвергнутых (по contentHashOf), либо в комментарии/UI пояснить, что rejected — это суммарное число отказов верификатора за все раунды, а не число уникальных плохих вопросов."
}

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- figma:figma-code-connect: Creates and maintains Figma Code Connect template files that map Figma components to code snippets. Use when the user mentions Code Connect, Figma component mapping, design-to-code translation, or asks to create/update .figma.ts or .figma.js files.
- figma:figma-create-new-file: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `create_new_file` tool call. NEVER call `create_new_file` directly without loading this skill first. Trigger whenever the user wants a new blank Figma file — a new design, FigJam, or Slides file — or when you need a fresh file before calling `use_figma`. Usage — /figma-create-new-file [editorType] [fileName] (e.g. /figma-create-new-file figjam My Whiteboard, /figma-create-new-file slides Q3 Review)
- figma:figma-generate-design: Use this skill alongside figma-use when the task involves translating an application page, view, or multi-section layout into Figma. Triggers: 'write to Figma', 'create in Figma from code', 'push page to Figma', 'take this app/page and build it in Figma', 'create a screen', 'build a landing page in Figma', 'update the Figma screen to match code', 'convert this modal/dialog/drawer/panel to Figma'. This is the preferred workflow skill whenever the user wants to build or update a full page, modal, dialog, drawer, sidebar, panel, or any composed multi-section view in Figma from code or a description. Discovers design system components, variables, and styles from Code Connect files, existing screens, and library search, then imports them and assembles views incrementally section-by-section using design system tokens instead of hardcoded values.
- figma:figma-generate-diagram: MANDATORY prerequisite — load this skill BEFORE every `generate_diagram` tool call. NEVER call `generate_diagram` directly without loading this skill first. Trigger whenever the user asks to create, generate, draw, render, sketch, or build a diagram — flowchart, architecture diagram, sequence diagram, ERD or entity-relationship diagram, state diagram or state machine, gantt chart, or timeline. Also trigger when the user mentions Mermaid syntax or wants a system architecture, decision tree, dependency graph, API call flow, auth handshake, schema, or pipeline visualized in FigJam. Routes to type-specific guidance, sets universal Mermaid constraints, and tells you when to use a different diagram type or skip the tool entirely (mindmaps, pie charts, class diagrams, etc.).
- figma:figma-generate-library: Build or update a professional-grade design system in Figma from a codebase. Use when the user wants to create variables/tokens, build component libraries, create individual components with proper variant sets and variable bindings, set up theming (light/dark modes), document foundations, or reconcile gaps between code and Figma. Also use when the user asks to create or generate any component in Figma — even a single one — since components require proper variable foundations, variant states, and design token bindings to be production-quality. This skill teaches WHAT to build and in WHAT ORDER — it complements the `figma-use` skill which teaches HOW to call the Plugin API. Both skills should be loaded together.
- figma:figma-swiftui: SwiftUI ↔ Figma translation. Use whenever the user mentions Swift, SwiftUI, iOS, iPhone, or iPad — in EITHER direction — translating a Figma design into SwiftUI (design → code), or pushing SwiftUI views / screens / tokens back into a Figma file (code → design). Triggers on phrases like 'implement this Figma design in SwiftUI', 'build this screen in Swift', 'push this SwiftUI view to Figma', 'mirror my Swift code in a Figma file', or whenever a Figma URL appears alongside `.swift` files / an `.xcodeproj`. Routes to a direction-specific reference doc; loads alongside `figma-use` for the code → design path.
- figma:figma-use: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `use_figma` tool call. NEVER call `use_figma` directly without loading this skill first. Skipping it causes common, hard-to-debug failures. Trigger whenever the user wants to perform a write action or a unique read action that requires JavaScript execution in the Figma file context — e.g. create/edit/delete nodes, set up variables or tokens, build components and variants, modify auto-layout or fills, bind variables to properties, or inspect file structure programmatically.
- figma:figma-use-figjam: This skill helps agents use Figma's use_figma MCP tool in the FigJam context. Can be used alongside figma-use which has foundational context for using the use_figma tool.
- figma:figma-use-slides: This skill helps agents use Figma's use_figma MCP tool in the Slides context. Can be used alongside figma-use which has foundational context for using the use_figma tool.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the actual code in `generateForCoverage`.
Let me read the full current implementation to verify the finding's claims about the `rejected` counter and `producedThisRound` logic.

The code matches the finding's description. Now let me adversarially analyze the actual claim.

The finding claims this is a "reporting defect" of `low` severity: `rejected` accumulates across rounds, so if a KC is chronically under-filled, the same deficit is regenerated in rounds 1..3 and each rejection is counted anew, inflating `rejected` relative to the number of unique bad questions.

Let me examine the logic carefully:

1. **Is `rejected` accumulated across rounds without reset?** Yes — declared at line 178 before the round loop, incremented at line 216 inside it, never reset. Confirmed.

2. **Is this actually a "defect"?** This is the crux. The finding's own claim is that `rejected` over-counts because "the same deficit is rejected multiple times." But let me think about what `rejected` semantically means.

Each round calls `aiService.generateKcQuestions` which generates *new* questions from the LLM (non-deterministic generation). The questions generated in round 2 are not literally the same question objects as round 1 — they are freshly generated by the LLM. A rejection in round 2 is a genuinely *new* generated-and-rejected question, not a re-count of a round-1 rejection. The verifier runs once per generated question per round. So `rejected` is correctly counting "total number of generated questions that failed verification across all rounds" — which is exactly what the field name conveys.

The finding's framing — "один и тот же дефицит может многократно отвергаться" / "the same deficit can be rejected multiple times" — conflates "the same deficit being targeted" with "the same question being rejected." The deficit being re-targeted does not mean the same *question* is rejected; each round generates and verifies distinct LLM outputs. There is no double-counting of any single rejected item.

So `rejected` = total verifier rejections across the whole operation, and `created` = total inserts. These are consistent: both are cumulative event counts over the entire multi-round operation. The ratio created/rejected is a meaningful quality signal (verifier pass rate). The finding even concedes there is no double-counting of `created` and no infinite loop.

The finding's `suggestedFix` "count rejected as a set of unique rejected (by contentHashOf)" would actually be *worse/wrong*: rejected questions never get a `contentHashOf` computed (it's only computed for accepted docs at line 239), and deduplicating rejections would understate the verifier's true workload/cost and distort the pass-rate signal.

The other suggested fix — "clarify in UI/comment that rejected is cumulative" — is a documentation nicety, not a correctness defect.

Let me quickly check how `rejected` is surfaced to the UI to confirm there's no actual misuse.

input.description: Find usages of rejected field in controllers, routes, and client

content.text:
The UI usage at line 143 confirms it: the message is `"Сгенерировано: +${res.created}, отклонено судьёй: ${res.rejected}"` — "Generated: +X, rejected by judge: Y". This phrasing describes exactly what the value is: the total number of generations rejected by the judge/verifier. It does not claim "unique bad questions." So the label already matches the cumulative semantics — there is no actual mismatch or misleading presentation.

The finding's premise is flawed:
- Each round generates fresh LLM questions; a rejection in a later round is a genuinely distinct generated item, not a re-count of an earlier rejection.
- `rejected` and `created` are both cumulative event counters over the whole operation, so they are mutually consistent and the pass-rate is meaningful.
- The UI label ("rejected by judge") already describes the cumulative count accurately — no misleading semantics.
- The suggested "unique by contentHashOf" fix is unworkable (hash is never computed for rejected items) and would distort the metric.

This is not a real correctness or integration defect.

input.reason:
Опровергнуто чтением реального кода (server/src/services/questionBank.service.ts:177-263 и client/src/components/ktp/TopicKnowledgeBank.tsx:143).

Премиса находки ошибочна. Утверждается, что «один и тот же дефицит отвергается многократно» и потому rejected завышен относительно числа уникальных плохих вопросов. Но каждый раунд вызывает aiService.generateKcQuestions (строки 199-206), который недетерминированно генерирует НОВЫЕ вопросы у LLM. Отказ в раунде 2 — это отказ другого, заново сгенерированного вопроса, а не повторный учёт того же отказа из раунда 1. Верификатор (строка 214) выполняется ровно один раз на каждый сгенерированный вопрос за раунд. Никакого двойного учёта одного и того же отвергнутого item'а нет. «Тот же дефицит снова берётся в цель» != «тот же вопрос снова отвергается».

Таким образом rejected (строка 216) и created (строка 244) — оба кумулятивные счётчики событий за всю операцию, взаимно согласованные; их отношение даёт осмысленный pass-rate верификатора. Сама находка признаёт отсутствие двойного учёта created и отсутствие бесконечного цикла.

«Отчётный дефект» тоже не подтверждается: UI (TopicKnowledgeBank.tsx:143) показывает «Сгенерировано: +{created}, отклонено судьёй: {rejected}» — формулировка «отклонено судьёй» точно описывает суммарное число отказов верификатора, она НЕ заявляет «уникальные плохие вопросы». Рассинхрона семантики и метки нет.

Предложенный фикс (считать rejected множеством уникальных по contentHashOf) нереализуем и был бы хуже: contentHashOf вычисляется только для принятых документов (строка 239), для отвергнутых вопросов хэш не считается; дедуп отказов исказил бы реальную нагрузку/стоимость верификатора и pass-rate. Второй вариант (пояснить в комментарии) — косметика, а не дефект корректности или интеграции.

По умолчанию при таком разборе — isReal=false.

content.text:
I verified this finding against the actual code and it is a false positive.

**Verdict: not a real defect (isReal=false).**

The finding's core premise is mistaken. It claims `rejected` is inflated because "the same deficit can be rejected multiple times." But:

- Each round calls `aiService.generateKcQuestions` (questionBank.service.ts:199-206), which generates **fresh, non-deterministic** LLM questions. A rejection in round 2 is a distinct newly-generated question failing verification — not a re-count of a round-1 rejection. The verifier (line 214) runs exactly once per generated question per round. There is no double-counting of any single rejected item. "Re-targeting the same deficit" is not the same as "re-rejecting the same question."

- `rejected` (line 216) and `created` (line 244) are both cumulative event counters over the whole operation and are mutually consistent; their ratio is a meaningful verifier pass-rate. The finding itself concedes there's no double-counting of `created` and no infinite loop.

- The "reporting defect" claim doesn't hold either: the UI (TopicKnowledgeBank.tsx:143) shows `"Сгенерировано: +{created}, отклонено судьёй: {rejected}"` — "rejected by judge," which accurately describes a cumulative count of verifier rejections. It never claims "unique bad questions," so there's no misleading semantics.

- The suggested fix (dedup `rejected` by `contentHashOf`) is unworkable — `contentHashOf` is only computed for accepted docs (line 239), never for rejected questions — and would distort the metric anyway.
